tools.name: add-to-context
tools.description:
Adds the object to the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are useful long-term for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: remove-from-context
tools.description:
Removes the object from the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are no longer useful for the conversation.

- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

response.delta: Here are all the available schemas and their typ

generic object type that allows additional properties. |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** |

Represents a function with name, description, input/output schemas, services, and bindings. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos

.type.organization`** | An organization with fields like name, description, status, image, website, and location. |
| 4
| **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact with fields like full name, emails, phone

numbers, addresses, job title, organization, and more. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A tag with
a label and optional hue (color), used for tagging/labeling other

objects. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | A trigger configuration that can fire
functions based on events like emails, queues, subscriptions, timers (cron), or webhooks. |
These are the six typenames currently available in the space
. Let me know if you'd like more details about any specific schema or want to create/query objects

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas/typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **`com.example.type.expando`** | A flexible/generic object type that allows additional properties. |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Represents a function with name, description, input/output schemas, services, and bindings. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | An organization with fields like name, description, status, image, website, and location. |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact with fields like full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, organization, and more. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A tag with a label and optional hue (color), used for tagging/labeling other objects. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | A trigger configuration that can fire functions based on events like emails, queues, subscriptions, timers (cron), or webhooks. |

These are the six typenames currently available in the space. Let me know if you'd like more details about any specific schema or want to create/query objects of a particular type!

content.content: Create an organization called "DXOS" and a person named "Alice".

response.delta: The user wants me to create two objects: an organization and a person. These are independent operations so
I can make both calls at once.

Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

params.success:
## DXOS — Research Summary

### Overview

<cite index="1-1,1-2">DXOS positions itself as "the new standard for building collaborative local-first software" and is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications.</cite> <cite index="9-1">DXOS stands for "Decentralized Operating System" — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.</cite> <cite index="1-3">DXOS applications run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite>

### Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="17-1">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="17-2">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="17-4">DXOS is a private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="17-3">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Company Size:** 11–50 employees (per LinkedIn).
- **Investors:** <cite index="17-6">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- <cite index="4-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite>

### Mission & Vision

<cite index="6-3,6-4">DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

<cite index="11-11,11-12">They are "setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks." Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

### Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK consists of three main integrated components:

1. **ECHO (Eventually Consistent Hierarchical Object Store):**
   <cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="25-16">ECHO is based on the Automerge CRDT, so both real time and offline collaboration is possible without manual conflict resolution.</cite>

2. **HALO:**
   <cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="25-9">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

3. **MESH:**
   <cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### Technical Architecture

- **CRDT-based Sync:** <cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge, a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>
- **Cloudless Design:** <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>
- **WebRTC Networking:** <cite index="1-19">Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- **Signaling:** <cite index="5-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>
- **Reactivity:** <cite index="1-20">UI components are re-rendered automatically as the underlying data changes, with minimal impact on performance.</cite>
- **SDKs:** <cite index="4-12">The three components are wrapped together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite>

### Flagship Product — Composer

<cite index="21-1,21-2">To show off the power of DXOS, they built Composer — a local-first workspace designed for small team collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="25-1">Composer is described as a "super-app for intelligence," where developers can collaborate in real-time, organize knowledge, extend with custom data structures, and run private machine learning and AI against their knowledge locally.</cite>

Key Composer features include:
- <cite index="21-13">Blending the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing users to build plugins, deploy custom functions, and leverage modern LLMs.</cite>
- <cite index="21-14">Real-time collaboration inside a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="21-18">Custom plugin building to enhance functionality.</cite>
- <cite index="21-22">A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="21-26,21-27">Everything inside of Composer is a plugin, meaning users can leverage core functionality or build out whatever they need.</cite>
- <cite index="21-38">Users can import and export their entire database as plain, human-readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>

<cite index="24-4,24-5">Composer is not yet ready for production; there are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite>

### Agents

<cite index="7-4,7-5,7-6,7-7,7-8">DXOS is a decentralized and privacy-preserving platform which communicates peer-to-peer. When all the peers go offline, it's impossible for any new peers to replicate. Agents solve this problem — they are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network, typically running on servers or desktop machines that are always plugged in.</cite>

<cite index="5-2">DXOS is also launching a fully-managed service to host agents</cite>, providing convenient off-site backup and persistent data availability.

### Open Source & Community

- <cite index="1-5">DXOS is fully open source.</cite>
- <cite index="3-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>
- <cite index="3-4">The primary codebase is the TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain, and Composer.</cite>
- <cite index="1-23">The DXOS Community is made up of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.</cite>

### Key Differentiators

- **Local-first:** <cite index="12-2,12-3">DXOS applications work offline, share state instantly when online, and leave end-users in control of their data and privacy.</cite>
- **Privacy-preserving:** All data stays on user devices; servers never have access to user data.
- **No centralized infrastructure required:** Eliminates cost and constraints of traditional cloud hosting.
- **Peer-to-peer collaboration:** <cite index="4-41">Latency is incredibly low with no server round-trips; peers can still communicate over a local network even without internet.</cite>
- **AI-enhanced:** Supports running private AI locally against user knowledge.

### Current Status

<cite index="12-6,12-7">The DXOS SDK is under active development, with the current focus on building features and functionality that enables the development of Composer. The team is currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK, and will prioritize suggestions and contributions that align with their work on Composer.</cite>

Adds the object to the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are useful long-term for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: remove-from-context
tools.description:
Removes the object from the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are no longer useful for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: delete-object
tools.description: Deletes the object.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update-object

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
Create an organization called "Cyberdyne Systems".

content.text: Let me first check what schemas are available to find the right type for an organization.
Let me first check what schemas are available for organizations.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: I found the `org.dxos.type.organization` schema. Let me create the organization.
I found the organization schema. Let me create the organization now.

content.content: I've created the organization **"Cyberdyne Systems"** successfully! It's now available in your space. Would you like to add any additional details to it, such as a description, website, or status?
Create a person named "John Connor".

response.delta: 've created the person **"John Connor"** successfully! Would you like to add any additional details, such as linking him to Cyberdyne Systems, adding
a job title, or any other information?

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas and their typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows additional properties |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function with input/output schemas, services, and bindings |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, website, location, etc. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with fields like full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, etc. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A tag with a label and optional hue (color) |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger that links to a function and can be activated by email, queue, subscription, timer (cron), or webhook |

These are the 6 typenames currently available in your space. Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to create/query objects of any type!

content.text: The user wants to create two objects: an organization and a person. These are independent operations so I can make both calls at once.

params.success:
# DXOS — Research Summary

## Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> The acronym stands for **Decentralized Operating System**, as noted on their GitHub organization page. <cite index="9-3,9-4">DXOS is a developer platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications.</cite>

## Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="9-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="9-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="9-8">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="9-7">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Team:** <cite index="4-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="9-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- **License:** Open-source under the MIT license.

## Core Philosophy: Local-First

DXOS champions the **local-first** software paradigm. <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite> <cite index="1-21,1-22">DXOS is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

## Core Technology Stack (SDK)

The DXOS SDK is built in TypeScript and consists of three main pillars:

### 1. ECHO (Database)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="12-7">ECHO serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="5-5">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Networking)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite> <cite index="1-19">It automatically synchronizes data across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>

### CRDT & Automerge
<cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite> <cite index="5-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>

### App Framework
<cite index="12-8">The SDK includes an App Framework to build modular applications based around plugins & capabilities, such as Composer.</cite>

## Flagship Product: Composer

<cite index="33-1,33-2">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite> <cite index="18-8,18-9">Composer is a local-first workspace designed for small team collaboration.</cite>

### Key Composer Features:
- <cite index="31-2">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions, and leverage modern LLMs.</cite>
- <cite index="33-5,33-6">Composer has real-time multiplayer implemented through a local-first model, which makes a lot of things better: from meeting notes to working on airplanes.</cite>
- <cite index="34-3,34-4">Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs; from the UI down to data and business logic, anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.</cite>
- <cite index="31-26">Leverage machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting your intellectual property.</cite>
- <cite index="31-27,31-28">Users own everything inside of Composer — no spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.</cite>
- <cite index="31-48">Import and export the entire database as plain, human-readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>
- <cite index="40-5">Scripts in Composer allow you to deploy custom functions that run on the edge.</cite>

### Composer Plugins:
- **Docs:** <cite index="40-3">A modern Markdown editor designed to provide native context to an AI assistant as you create new documents.</cite>
- **Sheets:** <cite index="40-9,40-10,40-11">A spreadsheet-style tabular interface for data manipulation that supports 300+ basic calculations along with support for custom functions, and is extendable via API calls and custom script integrations.</cite>
- **Sketch:** <cite index="31-46">Draw together in real-time and leverage sketches inside of programmable workflows.</cite>
- **Meetings:** <cite index="40-1">Built-in voice/video meetings with real-time transcription, task extraction & semantic CRM enhancements.</cite>

## DXOS Agents

<cite index="6-4,6-5,6-6,6-7,6-8">DXOS is a decentralized and privacy-preserving platform which communicates peer-to-peer. When all the peers go offline, it's impossible for any new peers to replicate. This is where agents come in. Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.</cite> <cite index="5-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite>

## Development & Community

- <cite index="3-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>
- <cite index="31-5">The community spans 8 cities worldwide, with 30+ contributors and 200+ community members.</cite>
- <cite index="12-4,12-5">The DXOS SDK is under active development, however the current focus is building features and functionality that enables the development of Composer. To this end, they are currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK.</cite>
- <cite index="16-11">The components are wrapped together in a set of SDKs (currently React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite>

## Key Links

- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer:** https://www.dxos.org/composer

You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
Create an organization called "Cyberdyne Systems".

content.text: Let me first check what schemas are available to find the right type for an organization.
Let me first check what schemas are available for organizations.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: I found the schema for organizations: `org.dxos.type.organization`. Let me create the organization.
I found the `org.dxos.type.organization` schema. Let me create the organization now.

content.content: I've created the organization **"Cyberdyne Systems"**. It's now available in your space. You can update it further with details like a description, status, website, or location if you'd like.
Create a person named "John Connor".

response.delta: 've created the person **"John Connor"**. Would you like to add any additional details, such as linking him
to Cyberdyne Systems, adding contact information, or anything else?

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:

- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

response.delta: me summarize the available schemas and their typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| #

object type that allows additional properties |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents

a function with input/output schemas, bindings, and services |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with

fields like name, description, status, website, location, etc. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact

with fields like full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, etc. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A tag with a label and optional hue (

color) |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger that can fire functions based on events (

email, feed, subscription, timer, or webhook) |

These are the 6 typ
enames currently available in your space. You can use them to create, query, and manage objects.
Let me know if you'd like to do anything with any of these!

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas and their typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows additional properties |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function with input/output schemas, bindings, and services |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, website, location, etc. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with fields like full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, etc. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A tag with a label and optional hue (color) |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger that can fire functions based on events (email, feed, subscription, timer, or webhook) |

These are the 6 typenames currently available in your space. You can use them to create, query, and manage objects. Let me know if you'd like to do anything with any of these!

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

params.success:
# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> <cite index="9-1">DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.</cite>

<cite index="5-3,5-4">DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

## Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="5-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="5-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="5-7">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="5-8">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Company Size:** <cite index="42-1">11-50 employees.</cite>
- **Organization Type:** <cite index="42-1">Nonprofit.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="5-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>

## Core Technology Stack

DXOS provides a comprehensive SDK built around three main pillars:

### 1. ECHO (Eventually Consistent Hierarchical Object Store)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="36-8,36-9,36-10,36-11,36-12,36-13,36-14">ECHO is a peer-to-peer graph database written in TypeScript. It provides secure, P2P data replication based on CRDTs, with no servers or central authority — all the data is with the clients. It offers connectivity with peers via WebRTC, support for multiple concurrent writers, collaboration on key-value objects and text, and support for offline writes and conflict resolution when peers rejoin the network.</cite>

<cite index="36-2,36-3,36-4">ECHO is designed to allow users to retain control over their data. ECHO is a secure storage mechanism, responsible for holding end-user data and identity information (keys, credentials, metadata, etc.) in persistent browser or filesystem storage.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity & Access Control)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="38-1,38-2">The HALO protocol supports the verification, transport, and exchange of identity information between networked peers. It provides public/private key-pair authentication mechanism integrated into ECHO.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Peer-to-Peer Networking)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite> <cite index="31-1,31-2">MESH is the set of peer networking technologies behind ECHO and HALO. MESH doesn't create a direct connection between each device, but maintains a network so that all devices can reach each other, perhaps through intermediaries.</cite>

### 4. EDGE
<cite index="34-3">EDGE provides web services running on Cloudflare workers, providing sync, backup, compute, etc.</cite>

## Key Technical Features

- **Local-First Architecture:** <cite index="22-8">DXOS provides instant-on app experiences with native support for local data storage that is secure, scalable and performant.</cite>
- **CRDT-Based Collaboration:** <cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge, a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>
- **Cloudless Design:** <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>
- **WebRTC Synchronization:** <cite index="1-19">Data automatically synchronizes across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- **Reactivity:** <cite index="1-20">UI components re-render automatically as the underlying data changes, with minimal impact on performance.</cite>
- **SDK Support:** <cite index="4-12">DXOS wraps its three main components together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite>
- **TypeScript Implementation:** <cite index="3-4">The platform provides a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.</cite>

## Composer — Flagship Application

<cite index="9-2">DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="25-1">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite>

<cite index="23-24">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside of a collaborative local-first workspace.</cite>

Key Composer features include:
- <cite index="23-30">Real-time collaboration inside of a modern markdown document editor.</cite>
- <cite index="23-15">Collaboration with threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- <cite index="23-17">Custom plugins to enhance functionality inside of Composer.</cite>
- <cite index="23-19">Custom schemas to work with structured data inside of Composer.</cite>
- <cite index="23-21">A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="23-37">Import and export of the entire database as plain, human-readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>
- <cite index="23-25,23-26">Everything inside of Composer is a plugin, meaning you can leverage core functionality or build out whatever you need to take programmatic workflows to the next level.</cite>

## DXOS Agents

<cite index="7-4,7-5,7-6,7-7,7-8">DXOS is a decentralized and privacy-preserving platform which communicates peer-to-peer. When all the peers go offline, it's impossible for any new peers to replicate. This is where agents come in. Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.</cite>

<cite index="6-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite> <cite index="7-13">If you don't want to run your own server, the managed service is a convenient alternative.</cite>

## Developer Tools

<cite index="12-1,12-2,12-3">DXOS provides debugging tools for DXOS Client in the Chrome developer console. Using this tool you can connect to DXOS Client to visualize and debug the state of the application.</cite>

## How Collaboration Works

<cite index="26-11,26-12,26-13,26-14,26-15">DXOS provides primitives and data structures: A space is an instance of an ECHO local database which replicates objects between a set of peers. An identity is roughly a public key that is used to identify users and their different devices. An invitation is a way members can invite new peers to join a space. ECHO is based on the Automerge CRDT, so both real time and offline collaboration is possible without manual conflict resolution.</cite>

<cite index="33-12,33-13,33-14">When different devices come online, they discover relevant peers in their spaces through a signaling service. Once discovered, peers exchange offers to establish peer-to-peer WebRTC connections and begin replicating user data. The DXOS networking technique forms a mesh network between peers optimized to minimize the number of connections while still maintaining connectivity and redundancy.</cite>

## Mission and Vision

<cite index="1-21,1-22">DXOS is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

<cite index="4-6,4-7">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries. Their goal is to enable developers to quickly and easily create powerful collaborative, interoperable applications.</cite>

## Community and Open Source

- **GitHub:** <cite index="3-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>
- <cite index="1-23">The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.</cite>
- Active community channels include GitHub, Discord, and BlueSky.

## Key Links

- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Composer:** https://www.dxos.org/composer
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
